title: Bad Chunking, Bad Retrieval
concept: A worked example showing how splitting mid-sentence or mid-table corrupts the meaning of a retrieved chunk.

exercises.prompt: In the previous lesson, we learned about 'overlap'. What is the main purpose of using an overlap window when chunking?

options.text: It makes the vector search run twice as fast.
It helps preserve context by sharing tokens across the boundaries of neighboring chunks.
It compresses the document size to save storage space in the vector database.

exercises.explain: An overlap window ensures that consecutive chunks share some tokens, preventing context from being completely severed at the boundaries.

cards.title: The Cost of a Bad Cut
cards.body: What happens when we split our data poorly? If we slice a document right in the middle of a critical sentence or a financial table, we feed incomplete, misleading puzzle pieces to our LLM.

cards.title: What is Bad Chunking?
cards.body: Bad chunking happens when text is split in ways that destroy structural meaning, such as cutting in the middle of a sentence, a code block, or a data table, leaving fragments that lose their original context.

cards.title: The Sliced Map Analogy
cards.body: Imagine cutting a physical map of a city into perfect squares. If a street name or highway is sliced right down the middle, a delivery driver looking at just one square won't be able to read the full street name and will get lost.

cards.title: A Dangerous Split
cards.body: If the sentence 'We do not recommend using Database A because it leaks data' is split after 'Database A', the first chunk reads 'We do not recommend using Database A'. The second chunk reads 'because it leaks data'. A search for 'leaks data' retrieves the second chunk, but the LLM won't know which database is leaking!

cards.title: LLMs Aren't Mind Readers
cards.body: Many believe that smart LLMs can always reconstruct the missing context of a broken sentence. In reality, if the retrieval step only fetches a fragmented chunk, the LLM has no way of knowing what was in the missing portion.

cards.title: Good vs. Bad Chunk Splits
cards.body: See how the location of the cut changes the usefulness of the retrieved text.

left.text: 'Chunk 1: ...use Database A. Chunk 2: Database A leaks data.' (Both chunks retain clear, complete meaning)

right.text: 'Chunk 1: ...use Database A because it. Chunk 2: leaks data.' (Chunk 2 has no subject and loses all meaning)

exercises.prompt: If the sentence 'You should NOT buy Product X' is split exactly before 'NOT', why does the second chunk ('NOT buy Product X') fail during search?

options.text: The chunk will be too long for the LLM's context window.
The chunk loses its context and cannot be matched accurately to relevant user queries.
The vector database will reject the chunk because it contains short words.
The chunk's characters will be scrambled during embedding creation.

exercises.explain: A chunk containing only 'not' is completely stripped of the subject and action, making it impossible for the embedding model to match it to a user's question about product recommendations.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Using a standard token overlap completely guarantees that complex tables will never be split or corrupted during chunking.
exercises.explain: Even with overlap, splitting mid-table can break structural syntax, and if the overlap isn't large enough, vital context like table headers will still be lost.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of why bad chunking harms retrieval:
exercises.sentence: When you split text in the middle of a sentence, you destroy the meaning of the retrieved chunk.
exercises.explain: Splitting text at arbitrary character limits without looking at boundaries can ruin the meaning, making it impossible for the LLM to answer correctly.

exercises.prompt: An LLM is fed a broken chunk that says: '...is not secure and leaks private user data.' Which response shows the AI hallucinating because of this bad chunk?

options.text: Based on the provided document, the warning states that Database X is prone to leaking data.
I cannot determine which database leaks data because the retrieved text fragment is cut off.
According to the system files, Database Null is highly dangerous and leaks data constantly.

exercises.explain: Option C shows a classic robotic hallucination pattern where it invents a fake database name ('Database Null') because the input chunk was cut off mid-sentence and lacked the actual subject.

exercises.prompt: Imagine a chunk boundary slices directly through the middle of a Markdown table containing employee salaries. Why does this ruin the LLM's answer when it reads only the second chunk?

options.text: The LLM will automatically query the vector database to retrieve the missing half of the table.
The semantic embedding vector will become longer, making the retrieval step faster.
The table formatting breaks, stripping away the column headers and leading to corrupted data analysis.
The embedding model will automatically repair the broken syntax using machine learning.

exercises.explain: If you split a dataset directly in the middle of a data structure like a markdown table, you destroy the structural syntax (like headers and column dividers). The LLM will receive fragmented rows without context, failing to extract the correct relationship.
